title: priority_queue
description: Not insertion order, not FIFO — always the highest-priority element first. Built on a heap, with a preview of switching to a min-heap.
objectives: Understand priority_queue as always returning the highest-priority element
Know priority_queue defaults to a max-heap
Get a first glimpse of switching to a min-heap

sections.text: queue always gives back elements in the exact order they arrived — first in, first out. priority_queue, from the same <queue> header, ignores arrival order entirely and always gives back whichever element currently has the highest priority.
priority_queue is a container adaptor that always keeps the highest-priority element accessible at the front, regardless of the order elements were inserted in.

Notice the elements were pushed in the order 30, 10, 50, 20 — completely unsorted — but they come back out in descending order: 50, 30, 20, 10. priority_queue reorganizes internally so top() is always the current maximum, no matter what order things were pushed.
5. Default Is a Max-Heap; a Preview of Min-Heap

sections.code: priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> minHeap; // now top() gives the SMALLEST

sections.text: This is only a preview — the full explanation of that vector<int> and greater<int> comes with Custom Comparators, right after this topic. For now, just know: by default, priority_queue's top() is always the largest value (a max-heap); adding greater<int> flips it to always return the smallest instead (a min-heap).

priority_queue is typically implemented as a binary heap, built on top of a vector — a structure organized so the maximum (or minimum) is always quickly reachable at the top, without needing every element to be fully sorted. This gives push() and pop() efficient logarithmic-time performance, much better than re-sorting an entire collection on every insertion.

sections.title: A hospital emergency room, not a checkout line
sections.text: Recall queue's checkout line, first come first served. An emergency room works differently: the most critical patient is treated next, regardless of who arrived first — a patient who just walked in with a life-threatening injury is seen before someone who's been waiting for a routine issue. That's exactly priority_queue: order of arrival doesn't matter, only priority does.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming priority_queue preserves insertion order like queue — it doesn't; only priority determines the order elements come out.
Forgetting the default is a max-heap — expecting the smallest element first without specifying greater<int> for a min-heap.
Expecting pop() to return the removed value — same as stack and queue, it only removes; call top() first if the value is needed.
Trying to access anything other than the current top element — like stack and queue, there's no indexing or iteration into the middle.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does priority_queue guarantee about the order elements come out in?
questions.answer: The highest-priority element (by default, the largest value) is always at the top, regardless of the order elements were inserted.

questions.question: Is priority_queue a max-heap or a min-heap by default?
questions.answer: A max-heap by default — top() returns the largest element unless configured otherwise.

questions.question: How is priority_queue typically implemented internally?
questions.answer: As a binary heap, usually built on top of a vector, giving efficient logarithmic-time push() and pop() without needing to fully sort every element.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: priority_queue always keeps the highest-priority element accessible through top(), completely ignoring the order elements were inserted in — by default that means the largest value, a max-heap. It's typically implemented as a binary heap over a vector, giving efficient logarithmic-time push() and pop() rather than requiring a full sort on every insertion. It can be configured as a min-heap instead using a custom comparator, covered fully in Custom Comparators. Unlike queue's first-come-first-served behavior, priority_queue behaves like a hospital emergency room: the most urgent element is always served next, regardless of arrival order.

sections.title: Trick to Remember priority_queue
sections.text: priority_queue = an emergency room, not a checkout line. Most critical (biggest, by default) goes first, no matter who arrived when.
